notes.note: A supplicatio was a formal public thanksgiving voted by the Senate for a general's victory; Cicero, governing Cilicia, had won one for his Amanus campaign and these are the days of thanksgiving being haggled over in Rome.

Days on which the assemblies (comitia) could meet to vote; the tribune Curio was being deprived of them, since a supplicatio of many days would consume the dates he needed for his own legislative business.
L. Aemilius Paullus, consul 50 BC and elder brother of the triumvir Lepidus; some quarrel of his had handed Curio an opening that Curio feared to lose by letting the thanksgiving be decreed.
L. Cornelius Balbus of Gades, Caesar's chief financial and political agent at Rome; he leaned on Curio not to obstruct, warning that to do so would injure Caesar's interest.
The Ides fell on the 13th of November; Pompey and the Senate wanted Caesar to lay down his Gallic command by that date, the crux of the looming crisis.

A man elected consul but not yet in office; the dispute was whether Caesar could stand for and be declared consul-elect while still holding his army and province, so as never to be a private citizen liable to prosecution.

The acta urbana or acta diurna, an official daily gazette of Senate votes, trials, games, funerals and city news, copied and sent to Romans abroad; Caelius arranged such a digest for Cicero in Cilicia.
